pattern: Compress the distinct x and y coordinates into a grid of elementary cells, then a cell counts once toward the area if any rectangle covers it, giving the union area before taking it modulo a large prime.
hints: Overlaps make naive summation wrong; instead think of the plane sliced by every rectangle edge into small cells.
Collect all distinct x coordinates and all distinct y coordinates; consecutive pairs define elementary rectangles whose area is known.
Mark each elementary cell covered if it lies inside any rectangle, sum the areas of covered cells, and reduce the total modulo one billion seven.

edge_inputs.description: three overlapping rectangles, union counted once
one enormous rectangle exercises the modulo
edge-sharing rectangles do not double count

stress.description: many small random rectangles over a bounded grid so overlaps are frequent
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    m = max(1, min(size, 40))
    rects = []
    for _ in range(m):
        x1 = rng.randint(0, 20)
        y1 = rng.randint(0, 20)
        x2 = x1 + rng.randint(1, 10)
        y2 = y1 + rng.randint(1, 10)
        rects.append([x1, y1, x2, y2])
    return [rects]
stress.note: small coordinate space forces heavy overlap, stressing union counting
